fr:mainmatter.html:p: We define the entropy of a random variable X \sim p_{X}, where p_{X} : \mathcal {X} \to [0, 1] as H(X) = -\sum _{x \in |\mathcal {X}|} p_{X}(x) \log p_{X}(x)
The 15 most recently modified trees, by git commit time.
Formal notebook for my journey in Stanford's CS336 as part of my LLM self-study endeavors.

Trivially, we can do character-based tokenization i.e. mapping each character to a unique number. For example, \mathsf {chr}(97) = \text {``a''} and \mathsf {ord}(\text {``a''}) = 97. The compression ratio will be > 1 because each token corresponds to a character potentially consisting of more than one byte.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. There is a fundamental tradeoff between compression ratio and vocabulary size. As an example, suppose we map every sequence of n bytes to a token. This would achieve a compression ratio of n. However, this would require 256^n tokens in our vocabulary.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Problem 2 was not entirely straight-forward to me. This actually reminds me of an idea from my information theory course, where we can assume we are given a "prior" over human language then we want to map low-probability characters to large strings in order to minimize the expected length (see huffman coding).

In byte-based tokenization, each byte is a token achieving a compression ratio of exactly 1, where a byte \mapsto [0, 256] . This is very elegant in the sense that our vocabulary size is fixed at 256.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. However, with this, the length of our encoding is the number of bytes in the string, which will become too long.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In word-based tokenization, we simply split the string into the corresponding words then map each of these to a unique integer.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. The vocabulary size is "unbounded" i.e. if we see a word we have not seen before then we need to extend the vocabulary size.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For the byte pair encoding (BPE), we instead train the tokenizer on raw text to automatically determine the vocabulary.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. In GPT2, they first split the raw text into words and then do this byte pair encoding.

Remark. There are a few additional implementation details to consider for project 1.
\mathsf {encode} loops over all merges. Only loop over merges that matter.
Detect and preserve special tokens (e.g. <|endoftext|>)
Use pre-tokenization (GPT2 regex)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We start by discussing a few essential questions one hopes to answer when they set out to train a large model.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. How long to train a 70b parameter model on 15T tokens on 1024 H100s?

Answer. Mysteriously, we spawn from the ether (will be explained later) that total_flops = 6 * 70e9 * 15e12. Intuitively, we know that we need to perform some computation for each parameter for each token. However, the 6 is still an ominous mystery. Given the total flops, we then can just take the GPUs FLOP/s multiply by the number of seconds in the day and divide total_flops by this number i.e. days = total_flops / flops_per_day

Remark. In practice, we must also consider the model flop utilization (MFU). Basically, our algorithm cannot utilize the entirety of the maximum FLOP/s provided by the hardware due to things like memory bandwidth, etc. To account for this in the calculation, they just take flops_per_day / 2.
Question. What is the largest model that you can train on 8 H100s using AdamW?

Answer. An H100 has 80gb of shared memory i.e. h100_bytes = 80e9. The calculation then proceeds as # parameters, gradients, optimizer state bytest_per_param = 4 + 4 + (4 + 4) num_parameters = (h100_bytes * 8) / bytes_per_parameter what we need each of parameters, gradients, optimizer state will become clear later.
Remark. In practice, we also need to account for activations, which will depend on both batch size and sequence length.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Tensor Memory. Tensors are stored as a floating point number. Memory is determined by (i) number of values in tensor and (ii) data type of each value.

float16: here, we decrease both the fraction and exponent bits. Importantly, there are only 5 exponent bits making the effective range (2^{-6}, 2^{6}). The dynamic range for small numbers is pretty bad. This can cause small numbers to "die" effectively killing these neurons for the remainder of learning due to backpropagation.
bfloat16: to fix the problems with float16, they instead keep 8 exponent bits and reduce the fraction bits to 7. This maintains the same dynamic range as float32 while still only using 16 bits.

fp8: Two variants
E4M3: has 4 exponent bits and 3 mantissa bits
E5M2: has 5 exponent bits and 2 mantissa bits Requires modern Hopper Architecture i.e. H100 to perform operations. Also supported by TPUs.

html:ol.html:li: float32 training works but requires lots of memory
Using lower precision data types allows you to train larger models due to lower memory requirements, but can cause instability
To optimize this tradeoff, use mixed-precision training.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In pytorch, tensors are contiguous blocks of memory, where their shape is controlled by strides. A stride basically just refers to how far you have to jump in physical memory to correspond to incrementing the logical index i.e. in a (2, 3) matrix each row requires jumping 3 spots in physical memory. Thus, stride[1] = 3.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Calling .view((m, n)) just changes the stride but does not modify the physical memory. If you call .view that results in a non-contiguous view, then you cannot call .view again because it assumes contiguous indexing to properly adjust the strides. To deal with this, they have .contiguous() and .reshape() is basically .contiguous().view().
Remark. All elementwise operations make a copy i.e. x + y. A useful function for masked attention is triu().

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
Einops is a useful python library to keep track of high-dimensional matrix operations i.e. matmuls over batch_size, seqlen, etc. High level overview
Initialize like X[float.tensor, "batch seq hidden"] = torch.tensor((2, 3, 4))
Can perform matmul with named dims like z = einsum(x, y, "batch seq1 hidden, batch seq2 hidden -> batch seq1 seq2")
reduce: x = reduce(x, "x y z -> x y 1") is just like mean(x, axis=-1)
rearrange: x = rearrange(x, "... (heads hidden1) -> ... heads hidden 1")

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We refer to the total number of floating point operatings as FLOPS. Alternatively, we refer to the number of floating point operations per second as FLOP/s.
Given a promised FLOP/s, the model flop utilization is given by \frac {\text {actual FLOP/s}}{\text {promised FLOP/s}}

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. Performing a matrix multiplication A \cdot B for A \in \mathbb {R}^{(B \times D)} and B \in \mathbb {R}^{(D \times K)}, requires 2 \times B \times D \times K FLOPS

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I hope to do a formal blog touring optimizers because I have written a paper on deep learning optimizers for my optimization course. Due to this, I will leave this blank for now. A brief list of the covered concepts are below:

html:ul.html:li: momentum
adagrad
RMSProp
Adam

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A key formula to remember: \text {total memory} \approx 4 \times (\text {params} + \text {activations} + \text {grad size} + \text {optimizer state size})

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Another useful trick is to use low precision for forward pass activations, then use float32 for gradients and network parameters.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Pre-norm vs. Post-norm. The first architecture variation discussed is when to apply the layer norm. As can be seen in the figure below, rather than apply the layer norm in the residual stream. They instead place it before the FFN and MHA layers.
They actually found that adding layer normalization before and after the MHA and FFN works the best. This is known as double norm.
RMSNorm vs. LayerNorm. Another useful trick is to use the RMSNorm instead of LayerNorm.

The RMS norm is simply the layer norm without the added bias term and centering i.e. \mathrm {RMSNorm}: \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \frac {\mathbf {x}}{\sqrt {\mathrm {Var}[\mathbf {x}_i] + \epsilon }}*\gamma . This is used to spare memory movement of layernorm, where the bias term \beta \in \mathbb {R}^d and has been found to be empirically almost if not as good.
One might wonder if matrix multiplies are the only thing that matters what can such a small change really accomplish.

html:ul.html:li: Due to memory movement, despite being .17% of FLOPS, provided 25% speedup in runtime!!

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Most people apparently just drop the bias term and keep the centering i.e. subtracting the mean. This makes sense because computing the mean does not require loading any additional information back to memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Despite a long list of activations, the two focused on are ReLU and GELU.

The rectified linear unit(ReLU) is defined as \mathrm {ReLU} : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \max (0, \mathbf {x}), where the \max is done elementwise i.e. \mathrm {ReLU}(\mathbf {x})_i = \max (0, x_i) This provides "nice" gradients, making it common when training neural networks.

The Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU) is a slight modification to the ReLU to account for the non-differentiability at 0. Namely, \mathrm {GELU}(\mathbf {x}) : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \mathbf {x} \cdot \psi (\mathbf {x}), where \psi (\mathbf {x}) = \mathrm {CDF}(\mathcal {N}(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {I}))

html:figure.html:figcaption: GELU vs. ReLU activation and derivative. Taken from here.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Swish activation is given by \mathrm {Swish}(\mathbf {x}) : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \mathbf {x} s(\mathbf {x}), where s : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d is the sigmoid function.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The extra computation required by GELU or Swish is a non-factor because FLOPS are dominated by matrix multiplication and there is no increase in memory pressure.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
A gated linear unit is an activation function combined with an elementwise multiplication i.e.
\mathrm {ReGLU} = \mathrm {ReLU}(\mathbf {x}) \otimes \mathbf {V} \mathbf {x},
\mathrm {SwiGLU} = \mathrm {Swish}(\mathbf {x}) \otimes \mathbf {V} \mathbf {x}, where \mathrm {ReLU} is the ReLU activation and \mathrm {Swish} is the Swish activation respectively.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The matrix \mathbf {V} introduces additional learnable parameters. Therefore, an architecture that uses gating typically reduces their other parameters by a factor of \frac {2}{3}.

fr:title.text: Computing FFN and MHA in parallel is free speedup

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Computing FFN and MHA in parallel is free speedup

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Another small trick is to parallelize computation. Traditionally, one computes the \mathrm {MHA} output and then passes this to the \mathrm {FFN} i.e. \mathbf {y} = \mathbf {x} + \mathrm {FFN}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x} + \mathrm {MHA}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x})))). However, this requires waiting for the \mathrm {MHA} to complete before performing the \mathrm {FFN} computation. It has been found that performing these in parallel does not cause any severe degradation in performance. Explicitly, instead they do \mathbf {y} = \mathbf {x} + \mathrm {FFN}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x})) + \mathrm {MHA}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x}))

fr:title.text: What is the role of weight decay in training large models?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What is the role of weight decay in training large models?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Since there is so much data, it is not feasible to train your model for multiple epochs. This is actually nice in the sense that we do not have to worry about overfitting and performing regularization.

However, many of the large models are still trained with weight decay. Tatsu claims that this is not to do with regularization but actually due to some weird interaction with the learning rate schedule. I am not sure I entirely understood this part.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Notation. For this section, our notion of an embedding is a function f that takes a token and a position i.e. f(x, i) is the embedding of token x, occurring at position i.

💡 Insight. The inner product of two embeddings should only need relative positioning i.e. for any two \mathbf {x}, \mathbf {y} with positions i and j respectively, there should exist some g, such that \langle f(\mathbf {x}, i), f(\mathbf {y}, j) \rangle = g(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {y}, i - j) Namely, this can be written as a function of just the relative positioning between the embeddings.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The simplest transformation that only preserves this relative information are rotations.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Rotating embeddings does not change relative position.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, rotations are only easily defined for \mathbb {R}^2. To get around this, they simply partition their input 2d slices and apply the rotation via \mathrm {ROT} = \begin {bmatrix} \cos m \theta _{1} & - \sin m \theta _{1} & & & \\ \sin m \theta _{1} & \cos m \theta _{1} & & & \\ & & \cos m \theta _{2} & -\sin m \theta _{2} & \\ & & \sin m \theta _{2} & \cos m \theta _{2} & \\ & & & & \ddots \end {bmatrix}
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The \theta _i's are fixed and chosen according to some schedule to capture different "frequencies".
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The embedding are used by appling them to the query and key matrices separately i.e. W_K = \mathrm {ROT}(W_K), \quad W_Q = \mathrm {ROT}(W_Q) These are then what is used in self-attention.
A lot of deep learning is empirical magic. A quick look behind the curtain into this magic awaits below...

html:ul.html:li: d_{\text {model}} is the input dimension
d_{\text {ff}} is the hidden dimension

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Exception 1. in the case of GLUs, we recall that to account for extra parameters we scale \frac {2}{3}. Therefore, d_{\text {ff}} = \frac {8}{3}d_{\text {model}}.
Exception 2. In T5, they use d_{\text {ff}} = 64d_{\text {model}}. The logic for this was that we could maximize the MFU by increasing matrix size. Ended up going back to small d_{\text {model}}.

html:ul.html:li: I believe the d_{\text {head}} is the output dim of each head.
This just says the total output dim for n heads is the same as if we did one head.
Means there is something important about splitting up the heads.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Rule 3. Aspect ratio = \frac {d_{\text {model}}}{n_{\text {layer}}} \approx 100-200
Pipeline dependent: if network speed is fast than parallelizing is easier and shallow networks are better.
If poor network speed then pipeline parallel might be more viable and deeper networks would be more parallelizable.

Rule 4. Vocab size
For single language models, vocab size is typically 30-50k
For multi language models, vocab size is typically 100-250k

The softmax is ill-behaved due to the exponentials. In the transformer, we have two softmaxes: one at the end and one in the attention.
For the first, note \log \sigma (\mathbf {x})_i = \log (x_i) - \log \underbrace {\sum _{j=1}^{d} e^{x_j}}_{D(\mathbf {x})} The only problem is the denominator term. The idea is to enforce the D(\mathbf {x}) = 1 by regularizing via a penalty on \log D(\mathbf {x}) ie. \mathcal {L}_{\text {aux}} = 10^{-4} \log ^2(D(\mathbf {x})) With the \nabla D(\mathbf {x}) should be 0 and we are effectively only considering the non-exponential term.
For the attention, they primarily operate on the logits prior to the softmax. One way is via layer norm before the softmax. Another is via Softcapping
Notes on the paper Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need.

For,
b batch dimension,
n sequence length,
d hidden dimension,
h number of heads. The total FLOPS for attention are roughly bnd^2. This comes from
3nd^2 FLOPS to compute Q = XW_Q, K = XW_K, V = XW_V.
Need to do this b times for each input in batch. and the total memory accesses are roughly \underbrace {bnd}_{X} + \underbrace {bhn^2}_{\text {softmax}} + \underbrace {d^2}_{\text {projection}}. This gives high arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {h}{d} + \frac {1}{bn}\right )^{-1}\right )

However, if we do incremental inference then we must multiply our total number of memory accesses by n i.e. bn^2d + nd^2. This gives arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {n}{d} + \frac {1}{b}\right )^{-1}\right ), which requires large batch and short sequence length.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. I believe we just ignore the softmax memory contribution in the inference case because it does not scale with n anymore as we are computing the logits just for the next token. Therefore, it becomes a \frac {d}{h} term, which we can safely ignore?
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The key is that the \frac {n}{d} term comes from the bn^2d term, where we are loading h (b \times n \times d / h) K and V matrices n times. Thus, we can improve the \frac {n}{d} term by a factor of h by simply not using a different K and V matrix for each head. This is the entire idea behind MQA.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Similar to the KV-cache, we still can use h Q matrices because we do not need to load n of them into memory because only the last one matters for inference.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Group query attention is the same idea as MQA but instead of using one K and V for every head, they use a group of them. Where obviously the number of groups needs to be less than the number of heads.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Grouped query uses subset of K and V matrices.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Also inspired by this idea of reducing the dependence of K and V on the sequence length is sliding window attention.

The idea behind sliding window attention is to ensure the number of weight matrices needed for the keys K and values V does not scale with the sequence length. Intuitively, this means allowing each word to "attend" to only some fixed number of previous words rather than the whole sequence

html:figure.html:figcaption: "the" only sees "on" and "sat" rather than the full sentence.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Pre-norm vs. Post-norm. The first architecture variation discussed is when to apply the layer norm. As can be seen in the figure below, rather than apply the layer norm in the residual stream. They instead place it before the FFN and MHA layers.
They actually found that adding layer normalization before and after the MHA and FFN works the best. This is known as double norm.
RMSNorm vs. LayerNorm. Another useful trick is to use the RMSNorm instead of LayerNorm.

The RMS norm is simply the layer norm without the added bias term and centering i.e. \mathrm {RMSNorm}: \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \frac {\mathbf {x}}{\sqrt {\mathrm {Var}[\mathbf {x}_i] + \epsilon }}*\gamma . This is used to spare memory movement of layernorm, where the bias term \beta \in \mathbb {R}^d and has been found to be empirically almost if not as good.
One might wonder if matrix multiplies are the only thing that matters what can such a small change really accomplish.

html:ul.html:li: Due to memory movement, despite being .17% of FLOPS, provided 25% speedup in runtime!!

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Most people apparently just drop the bias term and keep the centering i.e. subtracting the mean. This makes sense because computing the mean does not require loading any additional information back to memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Despite a long list of activations, the two focused on are ReLU and GELU.

The rectified linear unit(ReLU) is defined as \mathrm {ReLU} : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \max (0, \mathbf {x}), where the \max is done elementwise i.e. \mathrm {ReLU}(\mathbf {x})_i = \max (0, x_i) This provides "nice" gradients, making it common when training neural networks.

The Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU) is a slight modification to the ReLU to account for the non-differentiability at 0. Namely, \mathrm {GELU}(\mathbf {x}) : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \mathbf {x} \cdot \psi (\mathbf {x}), where \psi (\mathbf {x}) = \mathrm {CDF}(\mathcal {N}(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {I}))

html:figure.html:figcaption: GELU vs. ReLU activation and derivative. Taken from here.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Swish activation is given by \mathrm {Swish}(\mathbf {x}) : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \mathbf {x} s(\mathbf {x}), where s : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d is the sigmoid function.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The extra computation required by GELU or Swish is a non-factor because FLOPS are dominated by matrix multiplication and there is no increase in memory pressure.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
A gated linear unit is an activation function combined with an elementwise multiplication i.e.
\mathrm {ReGLU} = \mathrm {ReLU}(\mathbf {x}) \otimes \mathbf {V} \mathbf {x},
\mathrm {SwiGLU} = \mathrm {Swish}(\mathbf {x}) \otimes \mathbf {V} \mathbf {x}, where \mathrm {ReLU} is the ReLU activation and \mathrm {Swish} is the Swish activation respectively.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The matrix \mathbf {V} introduces additional learnable parameters. Therefore, an architecture that uses gating typically reduces their other parameters by a factor of \frac {2}{3}.

fr:title.text: Computing FFN and MHA in parallel is free speedup

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Computing FFN and MHA in parallel is free speedup

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Another small trick is to parallelize computation. Traditionally, one computes the \mathrm {MHA} output and then passes this to the \mathrm {FFN} i.e. \mathbf {y} = \mathbf {x} + \mathrm {FFN}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x} + \mathrm {MHA}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x})))). However, this requires waiting for the \mathrm {MHA} to complete before performing the \mathrm {FFN} computation. It has been found that performing these in parallel does not cause any severe degradation in performance. Explicitly, instead they do \mathbf {y} = \mathbf {x} + \mathrm {FFN}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x})) + \mathrm {MHA}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x}))

fr:title.text: What is the role of weight decay in training large models?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What is the role of weight decay in training large models?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Since there is so much data, it is not feasible to train your model for multiple epochs. This is actually nice in the sense that we do not have to worry about overfitting and performing regularization.

However, many of the large models are still trained with weight decay. Tatsu claims that this is not to do with regularization but actually due to some weird interaction with the learning rate schedule. I am not sure I entirely understood this part.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Notation. For this section, our notion of an embedding is a function f that takes a token and a position i.e. f(x, i) is the embedding of token x, occurring at position i.

💡 Insight. The inner product of two embeddings should only need relative positioning i.e. for any two \mathbf {x}, \mathbf {y} with positions i and j respectively, there should exist some g, such that \langle f(\mathbf {x}, i), f(\mathbf {y}, j) \rangle = g(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {y}, i - j) Namely, this can be written as a function of just the relative positioning between the embeddings.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The simplest transformation that only preserves this relative information are rotations.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Rotating embeddings does not change relative position.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, rotations are only easily defined for \mathbb {R}^2. To get around this, they simply partition their input 2d slices and apply the rotation via \mathrm {ROT} = \begin {bmatrix} \cos m \theta _{1} & - \sin m \theta _{1} & & & \\ \sin m \theta _{1} & \cos m \theta _{1} & & & \\ & & \cos m \theta _{2} & -\sin m \theta _{2} & \\ & & \sin m \theta _{2} & \cos m \theta _{2} & \\ & & & & \ddots \end {bmatrix}
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The \theta _i's are fixed and chosen according to some schedule to capture different "frequencies".
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The embedding are used by appling them to the query and key matrices separately i.e. W_K = \mathrm {ROT}(W_K), \quad W_Q = \mathrm {ROT}(W_Q) These are then what is used in self-attention.

fr:title.text: What is the role of weight decay in training large models?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What is the role of weight decay in training large models?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Since there is so much data, it is not feasible to train your model for multiple epochs. This is actually nice in the sense that we do not have to worry about overfitting and performing regularization.

However, many of the large models are still trained with weight decay. Tatsu claims that this is not to do with regularization but actually due to some weird interaction with the learning rate schedule. I am not sure I entirely understood this part.

The softmax is ill-behaved due to the exponentials. In the transformer, we have two softmaxes: one at the end and one in the attention.
For the first, note \log \sigma (\mathbf {x})_i = \log (x_i) - \log \underbrace {\sum _{j=1}^{d} e^{x_j}}_{D(\mathbf {x})} The only problem is the denominator term. The idea is to enforce the D(\mathbf {x}) = 1 by regularizing via a penalty on \log D(\mathbf {x}) ie. \mathcal {L}_{\text {aux}} = 10^{-4} \log ^2(D(\mathbf {x})) With the \nabla D(\mathbf {x}) should be 0 and we are effectively only considering the non-exponential term.
For the attention, they primarily operate on the logits prior to the softmax. One way is via layer norm before the softmax. Another is via Softcapping

Softcapping is a stability trick in the attention mechanism. It is a way to ensure that the softmax does not explode. \mathrm {logits} = \mathrm {softcap} \cdot \tanh \left ( \frac {\mathrm {logits}}{\mathrm {softcap}} \right )

fr:title.text: Computing FFN and MHA in parallel is free speedup

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Computing FFN and MHA in parallel is free speedup

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Another small trick is to parallelize computation. Traditionally, one computes the \mathrm {MHA} output and then passes this to the \mathrm {FFN} i.e. \mathbf {y} = \mathbf {x} + \mathrm {FFN}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x} + \mathrm {MHA}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x})))). However, this requires waiting for the \mathrm {MHA} to complete before performing the \mathrm {FFN} computation. It has been found that performing these in parallel does not cause any severe degradation in performance. Explicitly, instead they do \mathbf {y} = \mathbf {x} + \mathrm {FFN}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x})) + \mathrm {MHA}(\mathrm {LN}(\mathbf {x}))
Despite a long list of activations, the two focused on are ReLU and GELU.

The rectified linear unit(ReLU) is defined as \mathrm {ReLU} : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \max (0, \mathbf {x}), where the \max is done elementwise i.e. \mathrm {ReLU}(\mathbf {x})_i = \max (0, x_i) This provides "nice" gradients, making it common when training neural networks.

The Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU) is a slight modification to the ReLU to account for the non-differentiability at 0. Namely, \mathrm {GELU}(\mathbf {x}) : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \mathbf {x} \cdot \psi (\mathbf {x}), where \psi (\mathbf {x}) = \mathrm {CDF}(\mathcal {N}(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {I}))

html:figure.html:figcaption: GELU vs. ReLU activation and derivative. Taken from here.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The Swish activation is given by \mathrm {Swish}(\mathbf {x}) : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \mathbf {x} s(\mathbf {x}), where s : \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d is the sigmoid function.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
A gated linear unit is an activation function combined with an elementwise multiplication i.e.
\mathrm {ReGLU} = \mathrm {ReLU}(\mathbf {x}) \otimes \mathbf {V} \mathbf {x},
\mathrm {SwiGLU} = \mathrm {Swish}(\mathbf {x}) \otimes \mathbf {V} \mathbf {x}, where \mathrm {ReLU} is the ReLU activation and \mathrm {Swish} is the Swish activation respectively.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The matrix \mathbf {V} introduces additional learnable parameters. Therefore, an architecture that uses gating typically reduces their other parameters by a factor of \frac {2}{3}.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Pre-norm vs. Post-norm. The first architecture variation discussed is when to apply the layer norm. As can be seen in the figure below, rather than apply the layer norm in the residual stream. They instead place it before the FFN and MHA layers.
They actually found that adding layer normalization before and after the MHA and FFN works the best. This is known as double norm.
RMSNorm vs. LayerNorm. Another useful trick is to use the RMSNorm instead of LayerNorm.

The RMS norm is simply the layer norm without the added bias term and centering i.e. \mathrm {RMSNorm}: \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d, \quad \mathbf {x} \mapsto \frac {\mathbf {x}}{\sqrt {\mathrm {Var}[\mathbf {x}_i] + \epsilon }}*\gamma . This is used to spare memory movement of layernorm, where the bias term \beta \in \mathbb {R}^d and has been found to be empirically almost if not as good.
One might wonder if matrix multiplies are the only thing that matters what can such a small change really accomplish.

html:ul.html:li: Due to memory movement, despite being .17% of FLOPS, provided 25% speedup in runtime!!

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Most people apparently just drop the bias term and keep the centering i.e. subtracting the mean. This makes sense because computing the mean does not require loading any additional information back to memory.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Tensor Memory. Tensors are stored as a floating point number. Memory is determined by (i) number of values in tensor and (ii) data type of each value.

float16: here, we decrease both the fraction and exponent bits. Importantly, there are only 5 exponent bits making the effective range (2^{-6}, 2^{6}). The dynamic range for small numbers is pretty bad. This can cause small numbers to "die" effectively killing these neurons for the remainder of learning due to backpropagation.
bfloat16: to fix the problems with float16, they instead keep 8 exponent bits and reduce the fraction bits to 7. This maintains the same dynamic range as float32 while still only using 16 bits.

fp8: Two variants
E4M3: has 4 exponent bits and 3 mantissa bits
E5M2: has 5 exponent bits and 2 mantissa bits Requires modern Hopper Architecture i.e. H100 to perform operations. Also supported by TPUs.

html:ol.html:li: float32 training works but requires lots of memory
Using lower precision data types allows you to train larger models due to lower memory requirements, but can cause instability
To optimize this tradeoff, use mixed-precision training.

html:ul.html:li: d_{\text {model}} is the input dimension
d_{\text {ff}} is the hidden dimension

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Exception 1. in the case of GLUs, we recall that to account for extra parameters we scale \frac {2}{3}. Therefore, d_{\text {ff}} = \frac {8}{3}d_{\text {model}}.
Exception 2. In T5, they use d_{\text {ff}} = 64d_{\text {model}}. The logic for this was that we could maximize the MFU by increasing matrix size. Ended up going back to small d_{\text {model}}.

html:ul.html:li: I believe the d_{\text {head}} is the output dim of each head.
This just says the total output dim for n heads is the same as if we did one head.
Means there is something important about splitting up the heads.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Rule 3. Aspect ratio = \frac {d_{\text {model}}}{n_{\text {layer}}} \approx 100-200
Pipeline dependent: if network speed is fast than parallelizing is easier and shallow networks are better.
If poor network speed then pipeline parallel might be more viable and deeper networks would be more parallelizable.

Rule 4. Vocab size
For single language models, vocab size is typically 30-50k
For multi language models, vocab size is typically 100-250k
We define the entropy of a random variable X \sim p_{X}, where p_{X} : \mathcal {X} \to [0, 1] as H(X) = -\sum _{x \in |\mathcal {X}|} p_{X}(x) \log p_{X}(x)
The simplest indirect network: an n \times m grid of switch points connecting n inputs to m outputs. Any input can be connected to any free output simultaneously — non-blocking.

html:ul.html:li: One hop per transit (minimum possible).
Serializes if multiple senders target the same receiver.
Switch area grows as O(n \cdot m) — the dominant cost as systems grow.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Crossbar router area scales as the square of the port count, which is why high-radix routers are dramatically more expensive than low-radix ones.
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Crossbars are common as the inner switch of larger structured networks (Clos network, Fat tree) — large flat crossbars are impractical at scale, but small crossbars are cheap.

A three-stage indirect network parameterized by (m, n, r): r input switches and r output switches each of size n \times m, with m middle-stage switches each r \times r. Total terminal nodes N = nr. Non-blocking when m \geq 2n - 1.

html:ul.html:li: Number of total switching elements scales as N^{1.5} versus N^2 for a flat Crossbar while preserving non-blocking.
Path diversity is m — multiple distinct paths between any source and destination.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Proof. There are rmn input-switch elements, mr^2 middle-switch elements, and rmn output-switch elements: S = 2rmn + mr^2. With r = N/n: S = 2Nm + m(N/n)^2. Choosing n = \sqrt {N} and m \geq 2n - 1 = O(\sqrt {N}): S = 2mN + 2m\sqrt {N} = O(N^{1.5}).
Remark. A folded Clos network — input and output stages combined, making n input ports and m output ports bidirectional — is structurally the same as a Fat tree.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Replaces the calling Process's program with a new executable. exec() does not return on success — the original code, data, stack, and heap are discarded and the new program runs in their place. The PID is unchanged.
What exec() does internally:

html:ol.html:li: Loads the new executable's code and static data, overwriting the current.
Jumps to the new program's entry point with arguments passed as argv.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The canonical Unix pattern is fork() then exec() — the shell uses this to launch a command without becoming that command:
Redirection is implemented by opening a file in the child between fork() and exec(), attached to STDOUT_FILENO:
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The many variants — execl, execle, execlp, execv, execvp, execvP — differ in how they take arguments (list vs. vector), whether they use the PATH environment variable, and whether they take an explicit environment.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We define the entropy of a random variable X \sim p_{X}, where p_{X} : \mathcal {X} \to [0, 1] as H(X) = -\sum _{x \in |\mathcal {X}|} p_{X}(x) \log p_{X}(x)
C is said to be convex if for any x_1, x_2 \in C and \lambda \in [0,1] then \lambda x_1 + (1 - \lambda )x_2 \in C

A function f : C \to \mathbb {R} is said to be convex if C is convex, and for any x_1, x_2 \in C, \lambda \in [0,1], we have f(\lambda x_1 + (1 - \lambda )x_2) \leq \lambda f(x_1) + (1 - \lambda )f(x_2) and strictly convex if the inequality is strict.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. This can be thought of as the tangent is always an underestimate of a convex function.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If f: \mathbb {R}^n \to \mathbb {R} is twice-differentiable, then f is convex if and only if \nabla ^2 f(\boldsymbol {x}) \succeq 0.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. This implies positive curvature or the common "bowl-shaped" interpretation.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If \phi is a convex function on the range of X then \phi (\mathbb {E}[X]) \leq \mathbb {E}[\phi (X)]

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. When is D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P || Q) = 0?

Answer. Need Q(x) / P(x) to be constant almost surely. This occurs only when Q = P.
Q(x) / P(x) = c \implies Q(x) = P(x) c \iff \sum Q(x) = c \sum P(x) \implies c = 1

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For any pair of random variables, I(X;Y)\geq 0, with equality iff X and Y are independent.

This is Gibb's inequality in disguise: mutual information is the KL divergence between the joint distribution and the product of marginals, I(X;Y)\ =\ D_{\mathrm {KL}}\left (P_{XY}\,\|\,P_X P_Y\right )\ \geq \ 0, and Gibbs' inequality forces every KL divergence to be nonnegative, vanishing exactly when the two arguments coincide — i.e. when P_{XY}=P_XP_Y. It is the root fact from which Conditioning reduces entropy follows.
Observing another variable can only lower entropy on average: H(X\mid Y)\ \leq \ H(X), with equality iff X and Y are independent.

Immediate from Mutual information is nonnegative and the identity I(X;Y)=H(X)-H(X\mid Y): since I(X;Y)\geq 0, the conditional entropy cannot exceed the unconditional one. The caveat is that this holds on average over Y — a particular outcome Y=y can raise the entropy of X; it is only the expectation H(X\mid Y)=\mathbb {E}_Y[H(X\mid Y=y)] that is guaranteed to drop.
The joint entropy of a collection never exceeds the sum of the individual entropies: H(X_1,\dots ,X_n)\ \leq \ \sum _{i=1}^{n} H(X_i), with equality iff the X_i are mutually independent.

Expand the joint entropy by the chain rule, H(X_1,\dots ,X_n)=\sum _i H(X_i\mid X_1,\dots ,X_{i-1}), and apply Conditioning reduces entropy term by term: each H(X_i\mid X_{<i})\leq H(X_i). Conditioning on the past can only shrink each term, so dropping the conditioning gives an upper bound.

Over a finite alphabet \mathcal {X}, entropy is bounded by the log of the alphabet size, H(X)\ \leq \ \log |\mathcal {X}|, with equality iff X is uniform. No distribution on \mathcal {X} is more uncertain than the uniform one.

Again a face of Gibb's inequality: measuring P against the uniform law U(x)=1/|\mathcal {X}|, D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\,\|\,U)\ =\ \log |\mathcal {X}| - H(X)\ \geq \ 0, so H(X)\leq \log |\mathcal {X}|, and Gibbs' equality condition P=U pins down the maximizer. This is the discrete base case of the general maximum-entropy principle: absent constraints, the flattest distribution wins.
If a_{i},b_{i}>0 for i\in \{1,\ldots ,n\}; A=\sum _{i=1}^{n}a_{i},\quad B=\sum _{i=1}^{n}b_{i} Then, we have: \sum _{i=1}^{n}a_{i}\log a_{i}/b_{i}\geq A\log A/B
Consequences of Log-Sum Inequality. The log-sum inequality upgrades nonnegativity to convexity.

The KL divergence D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\,\|\,Q) is convex in the pair (P,Q) jointly: for any two pairs and \lambda \in [0,1], D_{\mathrm {KL}}\left (\lambda P_1+(1-\lambda )P_2\ \|\ \lambda Q_1+(1-\lambda )Q_2\right )\ \leq \ \lambda D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P_1\|Q_1)+(1-\lambda )D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P_2\|Q_2).

This drops out of the Log-Sum Inequality applied termwise. Joint convexity is the strong statement: it immediately gives individual convexity in either argument (fix the other and set the two copies equal), and — via D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\|U)=\log |\mathcal {X}|-H(P) against the fixed uniform law — it makes Entropy is concave a corollary.

Entropy is a concave functional of the distribution: for \lambda \in [0,1], H(\lambda P_1+(1-\lambda )P_2)\ \geq \ \lambda H(P_1)+(1-\lambda )H(P_2). Mixing two distributions never destroys uncertainty — the entropy of a mixture is at least the mixture of entropies.

This is the mirror image of KL divergence is jointly convex. Writing H(P)=\log |\mathcal {X}|-D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\,\|\,U) against the uniform law U, concavity of H is exactly convexity of P\mapsto D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\|U). Concavity is what makes the maximum in The uniform distribution maximizes entropy well-behaved — a concave objective on the simplex has a unique maximizer.

fr:title.text: Estimation and the data processing inequality

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Estimation and the data processing inequality

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The information-theoretic reading of a Markov chain: three variables form a chain U\to V\to W when W depends on U only through V, P(W=w\mid U=u, V=v)\ =\ P(W=w\mid V=v). Equivalently, U and W are conditionally independent given V, written (U\perp W)\mid V: P(U=u, W=w\mid V=v)\ =\ P(U=u\mid V=v)\,P(W=w\mid V=v).

Two consequences make this the right object for information theory. The relation is reversible — U\to V\to W \iff W\to V\to U — because conditional independence is symmetric in U and W. And any estimator forms a chain X\to Y\to \hat X: an estimate \hat X is computed from the observation Y alone, never touching X directly. That structure is exactly what the data processing inequality needs — you cannot manufacture information about X by processing Y.
Suppose that U \to V \to W. Then I(U; W) \leq I(U; V).
Intuition. by data processing, we can never create new information about U.

\{X_{n}:n\geq 1\}: a sequence of i.i.d. r.v.s. with mean \mu , variance \sigma ^{2}. Then,
\frac {1}{n}\sum _{i=1}^{n}X_{i}\stackrel {{p}}{{\rightarrow }}\mu

fr:title.text: Weak law of large numbers for functions

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Weak law of large numbers for functions

fr:mainmatter.html:p: \{X_{n}:n\geq 1\}: a sequence of i.i.d. r.v.s. with mean \mu , variance \sigma ^{2}. Let f:\mathcal {X}\rightarrow \mathbb {R} be any function, such that \mathrm {Var}(f(X))=\sigma _{f}^{2}<\infty . Then, \frac {1}{n} \sum _{i = 1}^{n} f(X_i) \stackrel {p}{\rightarrow } \mathbb {E}[f(X)]
Let X_{n}\stackrel {{p}}{{\rightarrow }}X, and f:\mathcal {X}\rightarrow \mathbb {R} denote a continuous function. Then, f(X_n) \stackrel {p}{\rightarrow } f(X)

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Remark. We have the following important properties
\mathbb {P}(A_{\epsilon }^{(n)})\geq 1-\epsilon {\text { for large enough }}n
|A_{c}^{(n)}|\leq 2^{n(H(X)+\epsilon )}
|A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}|\geq (1-\epsilon )2^{n(H(X)-\epsilon )}\;\text {for large enough }\;n\;, Together, this says that, depending on the entropy, the typical set can contain most of the probability despite being much smaller than the entire space.

html:ul.html:li: Contains almost all the probability.
Consists of \approx 2^{nH(X)}
Almost equiprobable sequences.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let B_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\subset \mathcal {X}^{n} be the smallest set with probability at least 1-\epsilon . Then, we must have
\mathbb {P}(A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\cap B_{\epsilon }^{(n)})\geq 1-2\epsilon

html:ul.html:li: Rate = \frac {\log _2 \theta }{n}
Probability of decoding error = \mathbb {P}|\hat {X}^n \neq X^n|

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
A positive real number R is said to be achievable if for all \epsilon > 0 (perturbation parameter) there exists a block length N(\epsilon ) such that \forall n > N(\epsilon ) the following holds
There exists an (n, \theta ) coding system such that \frac {\log \theta }{n} < R + \epsilon and \mathbb {P}|\hat {X}^n \neq X^n| < \epsilon .

Alternatively, a positive real number R is said to be achievable if there exists a sequence of coding systems (n, \theta ) such that \lim _{n \to \infty } \frac {\log \theta }{n} \to R and \lim _{n \to \infty } \mathbb {P}|\hat {X}^n \neq X^n| \to 0.
The minimum rate of source coding R_s is the infimum over all R such that R is achievable i.e. R_s \coloneqq \inf \{R \mid R \text { is achievable}\}

html:div.html:p: First we will show that H(X) is achievable. Then we will have R_s \leq H(X) by definition. Idea: take \theta = 2^{n(H(X) + \epsilon )} + 1. Give each element in typical set a unique index, everything else the same index. By properties of typical set, \mathbb {P}(\text {error}) < \epsilon for sufficiently large n.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A coding system (variable rate) with parameter n and an encoder-decoder pair (e,f), where e: \mathcal {X}^n \to \{0, 1\}^*, \quad \text {and} \quad f: \{0, 1\}^* \to \mathcal {X}^n We want the probability of error to be exactly zero; that is \mathbb {P}(X^n \neq f(e(X^n))) = 0.
Let \ell (x^n) denote the number of bits in e(x^n), where e is the encoding function. Then, the rate is \frac {\mathbb {E}[\ell (X^n)]}{n}

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Although it is unclear here, the difference between variable and fixed rate source coding is that we allow our encoder and decoder to map to/from \{0,1\}^* , where ^* is variable i.e. our encoded versions of our alphabet can have varying lengths.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If e is many-to-one then we say that e is singular. Similarly, if e is one-to-one then e is non-singular.

Given a code with n = 1, we define its extension e^*; that maps \mathcal {X}^* \to \{0,1\}^* by concatenation. That is e^*(x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots , x_m) = (e(x_{1}), e(x_{2}), \ldots , e(x_m)) A code with encoder e is said to be uniquely decodable if e^* is non-singular.
A code is said to be prefix-free or instantaneous if no codeword is a prefix of another.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Prefix free codes are uniquely decodable. To show unique-decodability, you simply have to provide a way to decode and show it is unique. For prefix-free, greedily decoding suffices.

Remark. Note that non-singular does not imply uniquely decodable. This is because for uniquely decodable we require the extension to be non-singular. For example, if we have that e(0) = 0, e(1) = 1, e(2) = 01. Then e^*(01) = e^*(2). From this, we can see that uniquely decodable is a much stronger condition.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let e: \mathcal {X} \to \{0,1\}^* denote a prefix-free code with lengths \ell _i = \ell (x_i) for x_i \in \mathcal {X}. Suppose |\mathcal {X}| = m. Then the following must be true: \sum _{i=1}^{m} 2^{-\ell _i} \leq 1
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The converse holds too: for any lengths \ell _i satisfying the inequality, one can construct a prefix-free code whose codewords have exactly those lengths.

Remark. Surprisingly, the constraint is no weaker for the larger class of uniquely decodable codes — every uniquely decodable code must also satisfy the inequality (McMillan). So allowing non-prefix-free-but-decodable codes buys nothing in achievable lengths.

fr:title.text: Performance Limit of Variable Rate Source Codes

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Performance Limit of Variable Rate Source Codes

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A variable rate code with parameter n is a prefix-free code e : \mathcal {X}^n \to \{0, 1\}^* , and a decoder f : \{0,1\}^* \to \mathcal {X}^n

A nonnegative number R is said to be achievable if for all \epsilon > 0, there exists an n(\epsilon ) such that for all n \geq n(\epsilon ), the following holds
There exists a variable rate code with \mathbb {E}[\ell (X^n)]/n = R and \mathbb {P}(X^n \neq f(e(X^n))) = 0 for all n.
The minimum rate of variable rate source coding is R_s \coloneqq \inf \{R \mid R \text { is achievable}\}

fr:title.text: Optimal variable-rate source coding rate equals entropy

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Optimal variable-rate source coding rate equals entropy

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Idea: map symbol x to interval in (0,1) using the CDF of X.

html:ul.html:li: Assume x_{1} < x_{2} < \cdots < x_m
X \sim P_X is \mathcal {X}-valued r.v. with \mathbf {p}_X
CDF of X = F_X(x) \coloneqq \sum _{x_i \leq x} P(x_i)
Midpoint function: G(x) = \sum _{x_i < x} P_X(x) + \frac {P_X(x)}{2} = F_X(x) - \frac {P_X(x)}{2}
Codeword: e(x) = \left \lfloor G(x) \right \rfloor _{\ell (x)}= first \ell (x) bits of G(x)

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Given x_1, \ldots x_m proceed as follows:
Take two x_i, x_j with smallest p(x_i), p(x_j) append 0 and 1 to their respective codes.
Combine them and treat them as a new value with p(x_i') = p(x_i) + p(x_j)
Repeat

Let X \sim P_X be an \mathcal {X}-valued random variable. Define L_H = \mathbb {E}_{X \sim P_X}[\ell _H(x)] and L^*(P_X) = \min _{\ell _1, \ldots , \ell _m} \sum _{i = 1}^{m} P_X(x_i) \ell _i. Then L_H = L^*(P_X).

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. SFE satisfies L_H < H(X) + \frac {2}{n} i.e. it achieves the optimal rate as n \to \infty . Huffman coding achieves optimality for all finite values n.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
The "type" \widehat {P}_{x^{n}} associated with x^{n}\in \mathcal {X}^{n} is the probability distribution on \mathcal {X} with pmf \widehat {\boldsymbol {p}}_{x^{n}} , defined as
\widehat {\boldsymbol {p}}_{x^{n}}(a)=\frac {N(a|x^{n})}{n},\quad \text {where}\quad N(a|x^{n}):=\sum _{i=1}^{n}\mathbf {1}_{x_{i}=a}.

For any n\geq 1 , let \mathcal {P}_{n} denote the set of all possible types over the alphabet \mathcal {X} with denominator n . That is,
\mathcal {P}_{n}=\{\widehat {P}_{x^{n}}:x^{n}\in \mathcal {X}^{n}\}.

For any type P\in \mathcal {P}_{n} , we can associate the type class T(P) , defined as
T(P)=\{x^{n}:\tilde {P}_{x^{n}}=P\}.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Example. Let \mathcal {X} = \{1,2,3\}. For n = 2, x^n = 12 the type is (\frac {1}{2},\frac {1}{2}, 0) and the type class is \{12, 21\} .

fr:title.text: The number of types is polynomial in n

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The number of types is polynomial in n

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Suppose |\mathcal {X}|=m , and \mathcal {P}_{n} denotes the set of all distinct types that can be constructed using elements of \mathcal {X}^{n} . Then, we have_
|\mathcal {P}_{n}|=\binom {n+m-1}{m-1}\leq (n+1)^{m}.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. \mathcal {X}^n has exponentially many elements in n. However, among the exponentially many elements they can only create polynomially many types in n for fixed m.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let P\in \mathcal {P}_{n} denote a type with denominator n . Then, the type class associated with P , defined as T(P)=\{x^{n}:\hat {P}_{x^n}=P\} satisfies
|T(P)|=\prod _{i=1}^{n}\binom {n-N_{i-1}}{N_{i}},\quad \text {where}\quad N_{0}=0,\text {and}\ N_{i}=n\times P(a_{i}),
for all a_{i}\in \mathcal {X}=\{a_{1},\ldots ,a_{m}\} .

fr:title.text: Sequence probability depends only on its type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Sequence probability depends only on its type

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Suppose X_{1},X_{2},\ldots ,X_{n}\stackrel {{i.i.d.}}{{\sim }}Q for an arbitrary distribution Q over the alphabet \mathcal {X} . Then, we have
\mathbb {P}(X^{n}=x^{n}) \equiv Q^{n}(x^{n}) = 2^{-n(H(\widehat {P}_{x^{n}})+D_{2n}(\widehat {P}_{x^{n}})=Q)}.
In other words, the probability that X^{n} takes a value x^{n} only depends on x^{n} through its type \widehat {P}_{x^{n}} . Furthermore, types that are far away from Q in relative entropy are exponentially less probable than those that are close to Q .
An immediate consequence of this is that if Q\in \mathcal {P}_{n} , and x^{n}\in T(Q) , then Q^{n}(x^{n})=2^{-nH(Q)} .

Let P\in \mathcal {P}_{n} , and T(P) denote its type class. Then, the following bounds hold:
(n+1)^{-m}2^{nH(P)}\leq |T(P)|\leq 2^{nH(P)}.

fr:title.text: The probability of a type class

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The probability of a type class

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let X^{n}=(X_{1},\ldots ,X_{n})\stackrel {{i.i.d.}}{{\sim }}Q for an arbitrary distribution Q on \mathcal {X} . Then, we have the following:
(n+1)^{-m}2^{-nD_{KL}(P|Q)}\leq Q^{n}(T(P))\leq 2^{-nD_{KL}(P|Q)}.
In other words, the probability that X^{n}\stackrel {{i.i.d.}}{{\sim }}Q lies in the type class of P\in \mathcal {P}_{n} is \approx 2^{-nD_{KL}(P||Q)} .

Let X^{n}\sim Q^{n} (i.e., the X 's are drawn i.i.d. from Q ), and E\subset \Delta _{m} denote a subset of pmfs over the finite alphabet X=\{a_{1},\ldots ,a_{m}\} . Let Q^{n}(E) denote the probability \mathbb {P}_{X^{n}\sim Q^{n}}(\widehat {P}_{X^{n}}\in E) . Then, we have
Q^{n}(E)\leq 2^{-nD_{\mathbb {E}}(P^{n} \parallel Q)},
where P^{*}=\inf _{P^{n}\in E}D_{\mathbb {E}}(P^{*}\mid Q) is the closest element of E to Q in terms of relative entropy. Furthermore, if E is the closure of its interior, we also have
\operatorname *{lim}_{n\to \infty }-{\frac {1}{n}}\log Q^{n}(E)=D_{K L}(P^{*}\parallel Q).

A binary hypothesis testing problem involves three components: the data X\sim P , the null hypothesis class \mathcal {P}_{0} , and the alternative distribution class \mathcal {P}_{1} , and the goal is to use the data to decide between the null and alternative; that is,
\text {given}\ X\sim P,\quad \text {decide between}\quad H_{0}:P\in \mathcal {P}_{0},\quad \text {versus}\quad H_{1}:P\in \mathcal {P}_{1}.
When {\cal P}_{0} and {\cal P}_{1} are singleton sets: that is {\cal P}_{0}=\{P_{0}\} and {\cal P}_{1}=\{P_{1}\} with known P_{0},P_{1} , then the problem is called simple hypothesis testing, and this is what we will focus on mostly in this course:
\text {given}\ X\sim P,\quad \text {decide between} \quad H_{0}:P=P_{0},\quad \text {versus}\quad H_{1}:P=P_{1}.

Given \alpha \in (0,1). The optimal hypothesis test in the Neyman-Pearson framework is one that satisfies \operatorname *{min}_{P_{Z\mid X}}\;\mathbb {P}_{H_{1}}\left (Z=0\right ),\quad \mathrm {subject\,to}\quad \mathbb {P}_{H_{0}}\left (Z=1\right )\leq \alpha .

Assume we have prior distributions (\pi _{0}, \pi _{1}) over the two hypotheses and there exists costs C_{1|0}, C_{0|1} associated with type I and type II error respectively. Then the Bayes optimal solution is the solution of \operatorname *{min}_{P_{Z|X}}\pi _{0}C_{1|0}\mathbb {P}_{H_{0}}(Z=1)+\pi _{1}C_{0|1}\mathbb {P}_{H_{1}}(Z=0).

Find a test that minimzes the maximum of the two errors i.e. \operatorname *{min}_{P_{Z|X}}\operatorname *{max}\left \{\mathbb {P}_{H_{0}}(Z=1),\ \mathbb {P}_{H_{1}}(Z=0)\right \}=\operatorname *{min}_{P_{Z|X}}\operatorname *{max}\left \{\pi _{1|0}(P_{Z|X}),\ \pi _{0|1}(P_{Z|X})\right \}.

Let X^{n}\stackrel {{i.i.d.}}{{\sim }}P_{X} , and consider a binary hypothesis testing problem with H_{0}:P_{X}=P_{0} versus H_{1}:P_{X}=P_{1}. Let (\pi _{0},\pi _{1}) denote the prior distribution of the two hypotheses being true. Then, the Bayes optimal hypothesis test with uniform loss is defined as
\Psi _{D}^{*}(X^{n})=\operatorname *{argmin}_{\Psi }\pi _{0}\pi _{10}(\Psi )+\pi _{1}\pi _{01}(\Psi )=\begin {cases}1&\text { if }L(X^{n})>\pi _{0}/\pi _{1}\\ 0&\text {otherwise.}\end {cases},

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
In the previous section, we consider the optimal test in the non-asymptotic regime i.e. for finite n what test minimizes the error according to some notion of error. In this section, we ask the question of what is the rate that this error converges to 0. More specifically, if \text {error}_n \leq 2^{-nV} then what is the best (largest) V we can hope to achieve? Kellen Kanarios2025326https://kellenkanarios.com/006G/006G/006G/Optimal Error Exponent in NP settingDefinition
We say that an error exponent V is achievable with type-1 error \alpha >0 , if there exist a sequence of tests (\Psi _{n}) with errors (\alpha _{n},\beta _{n}) , satisfying
\alpha _{n}\leq \alpha ,\quad \text {and}\quad -\frac {1}{n}\log \beta _{n}\geq V.
Let V_{\alpha }:=\sup \{V:V\text { is achievable with type-1 error }\alpha \} . The optimal error exponent is defined as V^{*}=\inf \{V_{\alpha }:\alpha >0\}=\lim _{\alpha \downarrow 0}V_{\alpha } .
Kellen Kanarios2025326https://kellenkanarios.com/006H/006H/006H/Stein's LemmaTheorem
For any \alpha >0 , we have V_{\alpha }=D_{KL}(P_{0}\parallel P_{1}) , which also implies that V^{*}=D_{KL}(P_{0}\parallel P_{1}) . In other words, the relative entropy between P_{0} and P_{1} is the optimal type-II error exponent associated with a binary hypothesis testing problem with H_{0}:P_{X}=P_{0} and H_{1}:P_{X}=P_{1} .
Kellen Kanarios2025326https://kellenkanarios.com/006I/006I/006I/Optimal Bayesian Error ExponentDefinition
Consider a binary Bayesian hypothesis testing problem with prior distribution (\pi _{0},\pi _{1}) . Then, we say that an exponent V>0 is achievable, if there exist a sequence of tests \{\Psi _{n}:n\geq 1\} , with probability of errors \{p_{n}(\Psi _{n})=\pi _{0}\psi _{0}(\Psi _{n})+\pi _{1}\psi _{01}(\Psi _{n}):n\geq 1\} , such that
\liminf _{n\to \infty }\frac {1}{n}\log \left (\frac {1}{p_{n}}\right )=\liminf _{n\to \infty }\frac {1}{n}\log \left (\frac {1}{\pi _{0}\psi _{10}(\Psi _{n})+\pi _{1}\psi _{01}(\Psi _{n})}\right )\geq V.
The optimal Bayesian error exponent for binary hypothesis testing with distributions P_{0} and P_{1} is defined as V^{*}\equiv V^{*}(P_{0},P_{1})=\sup \{V>0:V\text {is achievable}\} .
Kellen Kanarios2025326https://kellenkanarios.com/006J/006J/006J/Optimal Bayesian Error ExponentTheorem
For any \lambda \in [0,1] let P_{\lambda } denote the distribution over \mathcal {X} such that P_{\lambda }(x)\propto P_{0}^{\lambda }(x)\times P_{1}^{\lambda -1}(x) . Let \lambda ^{*}\in [0,1] denote the value at which D_{KL}(P_{\lambda ^{*}}\parallel P_{0})=D_{KL}(P_{\lambda ^{*}}\parallel P_{1}) . Then, the optimal error exponent in the Bayesian hypothesis testing problem with distributions P_{0} and P_{1} satisfies:
V^{*}(P_{0},P_{1})=D_{KL}(P_{\lambda ^{*}}\parallel P_{0})=D_{KL}(P_{\lambda ^{*}}\parallel P_{1}).

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
A sequence of fixed-rate source codes \{(e_{n},f_{n}):n \geq 1\} with parameters \{(n,2^{nR_{n}}):n \geq 1\} is said to be universal with rate R if the following are true:
The limiting rate satisfies \lim _{n\to \infty }R_{n}\leq R .
For any distribution P over the alphabet \mathcal {X} with H(P)<R , the probability of error, \mathbb {P}_{X^{n}\sim P^{n}} \left (f_{n}(e_{n}(X^{n})) \neq X^{n}\right )=0.

fr:title.text: Construction of universal fixed rated code

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Construction of universal fixed rated code

fr:title.text: The type-based fixed-rate code is universal

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The type-based fixed-rate code is universal

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The code constructed above is a universal fixed-rate code, such that \lim _{n\to \infty }\mathbb {P}_{X^{n}\sim P^{n}}(f_{n}(e_{n}(X^{n}))\neq X^{n})=0 for all P with H(P)<R .

fr:title.text: Redundancy of a mismatched code is the KL divergence

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Redundancy of a mismatched code is the KL divergence

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Suppose X\sim Q , but we encode it using an optimal code for P . Then, the redundancy incurred is approximately equal to D_{KL}(Q\parallel P) .

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. It will turn out the optimal choice of Q is the centroid associated with the k elements of \mathcal {P}.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: \mathcal {R}^*(\mathcal {P}) = C(\mathcal {P}) \coloneqq \sup _{\pi \in \Delta _k} I(W; X), where I(W; X) is the mutual information between W \sim \mathbf {\pi } and the output X, with X | (W = i) \sim P_i. The optimal Q^* is Q_{\pi }^* = \sum _{i = 1}^{k} \pi _i^* P_i.

The algorithm proceed as follows:
Initalize the dictionary to an empty set
Make one pass through the stream of data
Add every new "phrase" to the dictionary
Encode only the elements of the dictionary

The channel coding problem has three components — a channel encoder, a noisy channel, and a channel decoder. We pass a message W to the encoder to get X^n, which the noisy channel turns into Y^n; the decoder must recover W from Y^n. The encoder's job is to reduce redundancy while keeping codewords far enough apart that the channel noise cannot confuse them.

A code with parameter (M, n) i.e. (number of messages, number of channel users) consists of an encoder decoder pair
e : [M] \to \mathcal {X}^n
f: \mathcal {Y}^n \to [M]
The rate is \log _{2}\frac {M}{n} = (log of the number of distinct pairs)/(number of channel users)
P_{e, \text {max}} = \max _{1 \leq i \leq m}\mathbb {P}(f(Y^n) \neq i \mid W = i) - worst case over all transmitted messages prob of error.
P_{e} = \frac {1}{M}\sum _{i = 1}^{M} \mathbb {P}(f(Y^n) \neq i \mid W = i) - average prob of error.

We have the following components
\mathcal {X} - input alphabet |\mathcal {X}| < \infty
\mathcal {Y} - output alphabet |\mathcal {Y}| < \infty
P_{Y|X} - transition prob matrix

A rate R \geq 0 is achievable if
\lim _{n\to \infty } P_{e, \text {max}} = 0
\lim _{n \to \infty } \frac {\log _{2}M_n}{n} = R
The channel capacity is the maximum achievable rate i.e. C = \sup \{R \geq 0 \mid R \text { is achievable}\}

fr:mainmatter.html:div:
Remark. We make the following simplifying assumptions (for now)
We assume no feedback. This means that if we have W \to X_{1} \to Y_{1} \to X_{2} \to \cdots then X_i does not use the knowledge of previous channel outputs i.e. Y^{i - 1} \leftrightarrow (W, X^{i - 1}) \leftrightarrow X_i.
Random encoding: X^n(i) is drawn i.i.d from some specified distribution P_X.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The information channel capacity of a DMC (\mathcal {X}, \mathcal {Y}, P_{Y|X}) is defined as C(P_{Y|X}) = \max _{P_X}I(X; Y)
Non rigorous: says that channel capacity and information channel capacity are equal.

A channel is symmetric if
each row is a permutation of the first row
each column is a permutation of the first column
It is weakly symmetric if it satisfies 1 but the columns only sum to the same value.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. The capacity of a symmetric or weakly symmetric channel is \log (|\mathcal {Y}|) - H(r), where r is the first row of P_{Y|X}.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
A channel (with memory) is a sequence of conditional distributions:_
P_{Y_{1}|X_{1}},P_{Y_{2}|Y_{1},X^{2}},P_{Y_{3}|Y^{2},X^{3}},\ldots ,P_{Y_{n}|Y^{n-1},X^{n}},
where P_{Y_{n}|Y^{n-1},X^{n}} is a collection of distributions on \mathcal {Y} one for every (y^{n-1},x^{n})\in \mathcal {Y}^{n-1}\times \mathcal {X}^{n} .

An encoder (with feedback) with parameter (n,\Theta ) for a channel is a vector of mappings e_{i} for i=1,2,\ldots ,n , where
e_{i}:\{1,2,\ldots ,\Theta \}\times \mathcal {Y}^{i-1}\to \mathcal {X}.

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. We can decompose the joint distribution P_{X^n, Y^n} in terms of the what depends on the channel and the what depends on the encoder. Namely, P_{X^n, Y^n} = \prod _{i=1}^{n} P_{X_i | X^{i - 1}, Y^{i - 1}} \prod _{i=1}^{n} P_{Y_i | X^{i}, Y^{i - 1}} Note that the first term depends only on the encoder and the second term depends only on the channel.

html:ul.html:li: The encoder is a deterministic function of \mathbf {W}
The channel does not have access to the message \mathbf {W}.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
The differential entropy h(X) of a continuous random variable X with density f(x) is defined as
h(X)=-\int _{S}f(x)\log f(x)\,dx,
where S is the support set of the random variable.

fr:title.text: Typical Set for Continuous Random Variables

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Typical Set for Continuous Random Variables

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
For \epsilon >0 and any n , we define the typical set A_{\epsilon }^{(n)} with respect to f(x) as follows:
A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}=\left \{(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n})\in S^{n}:\left |-\frac {1}{n}\log f(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n})-h(X)\right |\leq \epsilon \right \},
where f(x_{1},x_{2},\ldots ,x_{n})=\prod _{i=1}^{n}f(x_{i}) .
The volume Vol( A ) of a set A\subset \mathcal {R}^{n} is defined as \text {Vol}(A)=\int _{A}dx_{1}\,dx_{2}\,\cdots \,dx_{n}.

fr:title.text: Properties of the typical set for continuous variables

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Properties of the typical set for continuous variables

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
The typical set A_{\epsilon }^{(n)} has the following properties:
\Pr \left (A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\right )>1-\epsilon for n sufficiently large.
\mathrm {Vol}\left (A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\right )\leq 2^{n(h(X)+\epsilon )} for all n .
\mathrm {Vol}\left (A_{\epsilon }^{(n)}\right )\geq (1-\epsilon )2^{n(h(X)-\epsilon )} for n sufficiently large.

For, p_{i}=\int _{i\,\Delta }^{(i+1)\,\Delta }f(x)\,d x=f(x_{i})\Delta \,. We define the discrete approximation of the differential entropy as \begin {align*} H(X^{\Delta })&\coloneqq -\sum _{-\infty }^{\infty }p_{i}\log p_{i} \\ &=-\sum _{-\infty }^{\infty }f(x_{i})\Delta \log (f(x_{i})\Delta ) \\ &=-\sum \Delta f(x_{i})\log f(x_{i})-\sum f(x_{i})\Delta \log \Delta \\ &=-\sum \Delta f(x_{i})\log f(x_{i})-\log \Delta , .\end {align*}

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Taking \Delta = 2^{-n}, then X^{\Delta } is an "n-bit" approximation of X.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Let X be any real valued random variable, and let X^{(1/n)} denote the discretization of X with parameter \Delta =1/n . Then, the information dimension associated with X is defined as
d=\lim _{n\to \infty }\frac {H(X^{(1/n)})}{\log n},

Entropy is a concave functional of the distribution: for \lambda \in [0,1], H(\lambda P_1+(1-\lambda )P_2)\ \geq \ \lambda H(P_1)+(1-\lambda )H(P_2). Mixing two distributions never destroys uncertainty — the entropy of a mixture is at least the mixture of entropies.

This is the mirror image of KL divergence is jointly convex. Writing H(P)=\log |\mathcal {X}|-D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\,\|\,U) against the uniform law U, concavity of H is exactly convexity of P\mapsto D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\|U). Concavity is what makes the maximum in The uniform distribution maximizes entropy well-behaved — a concave objective on the simplex has a unique maximizer.

Over a finite alphabet \mathcal {X}, entropy is bounded by the log of the alphabet size, H(X)\ \leq \ \log |\mathcal {X}|, with equality iff X is uniform. No distribution on \mathcal {X} is more uncertain than the uniform one.

Again a face of Gibb's inequality: measuring P against the uniform law U(x)=1/|\mathcal {X}|, D_{\mathrm {KL}}(P\,\|\,U)\ =\ \log |\mathcal {X}| - H(X)\ \geq \ 0, so H(X)\leq \log |\mathcal {X}|, and Gibbs' equality condition P=U pins down the maximizer. This is the discrete base case of the general maximum-entropy principle: absent constraints, the flattest distribution wins.
The joint entropy of a collection never exceeds the sum of the individual entropies: H(X_1,\dots ,X_n)\ \leq \ \sum _{i=1}^{n} H(X_i), with equality iff the X_i are mutually independent.

Expand the joint entropy by the chain rule, H(X_1,\dots ,X_n)=\sum _i H(X_i\mid X_1,\dots ,X_{i-1}), and apply Conditioning reduces entropy term by term: each H(X_i\mid X_{<i})\leq H(X_i). Conditioning on the past can only shrink each term, so dropping the conditioning gives an upper bound.
Observing another variable can only lower entropy on average: H(X\mid Y)\ \leq \ H(X), with equality iff X and Y are independent.

Immediate from Mutual information is nonnegative and the identity I(X;Y)=H(X)-H(X\mid Y): since I(X;Y)\geq 0, the conditional entropy cannot exceed the unconditional one. The caveat is that this holds on average over Y — a particular outcome Y=y can raise the entropy of X; it is only the expectation H(X\mid Y)=\mathbb {E}_Y[H(X\mid Y=y)] that is guaranteed to drop.
